needs.want: 'I want' is how you ask for something you would like to have.
You say it when you see something you wish for — a snack, a toy, a turn.
Try it like this: 'I want juice' or 'I want that one.'
needs.like: 'I like' tells someone that something makes you happy.
You say it about things you enjoy — a food, a song, a person, a game.
Say it like this: 'I like dogs' or 'I like this book.'
needs.dont_like: 'I don't like' tells someone that something does not make you happy.
You say it when something bothers you or you would rather not have it.
Say it like this: 'I don't like that' or 'I don't like loud noises.'
needs.all_done: 'All done' means you are finished with something.
You say it when you want to stop — done eating, done playing, done with a task.
Try it like this: 'I'm all done' or just 'All done!'
needs.yes: 'Yes' means you agree, or that you do want something.
You say it to answer a question when the answer is good with you.
Someone asks 'Do you want more?' and you can say 'Yes!'
needs.no: 'No' means you do not want something, or that the answer is not so.
You say it to stop something or to turn an offer down.
Someone asks 'Do you want this?' and you can say 'No.'
needs.please: 'Please' is a kind word you add when you ask for something.
You say it to be polite, so your ask sounds gentle and nice.
Try it like this: 'More, please' or 'Can I have that, please?'
needs.thank_you: 'Thank you' is how you tell someone you are glad for what they did.
You say it when someone gives you something or helps you.
Say it like this: 'Thank you!' after someone hands you a snack.
needs.mine: You say it about your own things — your toy, your cup, your spot.
Try it like this: 'That's mine' or 'Mine, please.'
needs.bathroom: 'Bathroom' is the room where you go potty and wash your hands.
You say it when you need to use the potty.
Try it like this: 'I need the bathroom' or 'Bathroom, please.'
needs.up: 'Up' means toward the sky, or higher than where you are now.
You say it when you want to go higher or be picked up.
Try it like this: 'Up, please!' when you want to be lifted.
needs.down: 'Down' means toward the floor, or lower than where you are now.
You say it when you want to get down or put something low.
Try it like this: 'Down, please' when you want to get off a lap.
needs.in: You say it when something goes into a box, a cup, or a room.
Try it like this: 'Put it in' or 'I'm in!'
needs.out: 'Out' means not inside — leaving, or taking something out.
You say it when you want to go outside or take something out.
Try it like this: 'Let's go out' or 'Take it out.'
needs.here: 'Here' means this spot, close to you.
You say it to show where something is or to call someone over.
Try it like this: 'Come here' or 'Put it here.'
needs.on: 'On' means resting on top of something.
You say it when something sits on a table, a chair, or the floor.
Try it like this: 'Put it on' or 'It's on the table.'
needs.under: 'Under' means below something, with that thing over the top.
You say it when something is beneath a table, a blanket, or a bed.
Try it like this: 'It's under the chair.'
needs.over: 'Over' means above something, or crossing the top of it.
You say it when one thing is above another, or going across.
Try it like this: 'The bird flew over' or 'Hold it over here.'
needs.behind: 'Behind' means at the back of something, where you can't see it.
You say it when something is hiding in back of another thing.
Try it like this: 'It's behind the couch.'
needs.next_to: 'Next to' means right beside something, side by side.
You say it when two things are close together.
Try it like this: 'Sit next to me.'
needs.between: 'Between' means in the middle, with one thing on each side.
You say it when something is in the space between two others.
Try it like this: 'It's between the books.'
needs.prep.at: 'At' points to a place or a spot.
You say it to tell where someone or something is.
Try it like this: 'I'm at home' or 'Look at me.'
needs.prep.from: 'From' tells where something started or where it came.
You say it about where a thing or a person comes.
Try it like this: 'It's from Grandma' or 'I came from school.'
needs.prep.of: 'Of' links two words to show they go together.
You hear it in the middle of lots of little phrases.
Try it like this: 'a cup of milk' or 'a piece of cake.'
needs.prep.through: 'Through' means going in one side and out the other.
You say it when you pass all the way across or inside something.
Try it like this: 'Go through the door' or 'Look through the window.'
needs.prep.around: 'Around' means on every side, or going in a circle.
You say it when something circles or is all about a place.
Try it like this: 'Run around' or 'Look around.'
needs.prep.inside: 'Inside' means in the middle of something, not out in the open.
You say it when you are indoors or something is within a thing.
Try it like this: 'Let's play inside' or 'It's inside the bag.'
needs.prep.outside: 'Outside' means out in the open air, not indoors.
You say it when you want to go out or something is outdoors.
Try it like this: 'Let's go outside!'
needs.direction.left: 'Left' is one of your two sides — the same side as your left hand.
You say it to tell which way to turn or look.
Try it like this: 'Turn left' or 'It's on the left.'
needs.direction.right: 'Right' is your other side — the same side as your right hand.
You say it to tell which way to turn or look.
Try it like this: 'Turn right' or 'It's on the right.'
needs.direction.top: 'Top' is the highest part of something.
You say it about the part way up high.
Try it like this: 'It's on top' or 'the top shelf.'
needs.direction.bottom: 'Bottom' is the lowest part of something.
You say it about the part way down low.
Try it like this: 'It's on the bottom' or 'the bottom shelf.'
needs.direction.middle: 'Middle' is the center, with the same amount on each side.
You say it about the part right in between.
Try it like this: 'I sit in the middle.'
needs.direction.front: 'Front' is the part that faces forward, the side you see first.
You say it about the part that leads the way.
Try it like this: 'I'm in the front' or 'the front door.'
needs.direction.back: 'Back' is the part that faces away, behind the front.
You say it about the part in the rear.
Try it like this: 'I sit in the back' or 'the back door.'
needs.direction.side: 'Side' is the part along the edge, not the front or back.
You say it about the left or right part of something.
Try it like this: 'Scoot to the side' or 'by my side.'
people.pronouns.i: 'I' is the word you use when you talk about yourself.
You say it before you tell what you do, want, or feel.
Try it like this: 'I want it' or 'I see you.'
people.pronouns.you: 'You' is the word for the person you are talking to.
You say it when you mean the other person.
Try it like this: 'You did it!' or 'I see you.'
people.pronouns.my: 'My' shows that something belongs to you.
You say it right before the thing that is yours.
Try it like this: 'my cup' or 'my turn.'
people.pronouns.your: 'Your' shows that something belongs to the person you're talking to.
You say it right before their thing.
Try it like this: 'your shoe' or 'your turn.'
people.pronouns.we: 'We' means you and someone else together.
You say it when you and at least one other person do something.
Try it like this: 'We go!' or 'We did it.'
people.pronouns.they: 'They' means more than one other person or thing.
You say it about a group you're talking about.
Try it like this: 'They are here' or 'They went home.'
people.pronouns.he: 'He' is the word for one boy or man you're talking about.
You say it instead of saying his name again.
Try it like this: 'He is my friend.'
people.pronouns.she: 'She' is the word for one girl or woman you're talking about.
You say it instead of saying her name again.
Try it like this: 'She is my friend.'
people.pronouns.more.him: 'Him' is the word for a boy or man when something happens to him.
You say it after an action word.
Try it like this: 'I see him' or 'Give it to him.'
people.pronouns.more.her: 'Her' is the word for a girl or woman when something happens to her.
You say it after an action word, or to show something is hers.
Try it like this: 'I see her' or 'her cup.'
people.pronouns.more.his: 'His' shows that something belongs to a boy or man.
You say it right before his thing.
Try it like this: 'his shoe' or 'his turn.'
people.pronouns.more.hers: 'Hers' means something belongs to a girl or woman.
You say it when the thing is hers, at the end of a sentence.
Try it like this: 'That one is hers.'
people.pronouns.more.these: 'These' means more than one thing that is close to you.
You say it pointing to things right here.
Try it like this: 'I want these' or 'these are mine.'
people.pronouns.more.those: 'Those' means more than one thing that is farther away.
You say it pointing to things over there.
Try it like this: 'I want those' or 'those ones.'
needs.asking.what: 'What' asks about a thing — what it is or what is happening.
You say it when you want to know about something.
Try it like this: 'What is that?' or 'What's this?'
needs.asking.where: You say it when you want to know where something is.
Try it like this: 'Where is it?' or 'Where are we going?'
needs.asking.who: You say it when you want to know which person.
Try it like this: 'Who is that?' or 'Who did it?'
needs.asking.why: You say it when you want to know how come something happened.
Try it like this: 'Why?' or 'Why did it stop?'
needs.asking.when: You say it when you want to know what time something happens.
Try it like this: 'When do we go?' or 'When is snack?'
needs.asking.how: 'How' asks the way something is done.
You say it when you want to know how something works.
Try it like this: 'How does it work?' or 'How do I do it?'
needs.linking.is: 'Is' tells what something does or what it's like, right now.
You say it to join a thing to what it is.
Try it like this: 'It is big' or 'Mom is here.'
needs.linking.can: 'Can' means you are able to do something.
You say it before an action you're able to do.
Try it like this: 'I can do it' or 'Can I have one?'
needs.linking.will: 'Will' tells about something that is going to happen.
You say it before an action that comes later.
Try it like this: 'I will go' or 'It will be fun.'
needs.linking.a: 'A' is a tiny word you put before a thing — one of something.
You say it right before a naming word.
Try it like this: 'a dog' or 'I want a cookie.'
needs.linking.the: 'The' is a tiny word you put before a certain thing.
You say it before the one you mean.
Try it like this: 'the dog' or 'open the door.'
needs.linking.this: 'This' means the thing right here, close to you.
You say it pointing to something near.
Try it like this: 'I want this' or 'this one.'
needs.linking.that: 'That' means the thing over there, farther away.
You say it pointing to something not so close.
Try it like this: 'I want that' or 'that one.'
needs.linking.it: 'It' is the word you use for a thing instead of its name.
You say it once everyone knows what you mean.
Try it like this: 'I see it' or 'It is fun.'
needs.linking.and: You say it to add one more thing.
Try it like this: 'you and me' or 'milk and cookies.'
needs.linking.but: 'But' shows that the next part is different from what came before.
You say it when there's a change or a problem.
Try it like this: 'I want it, but it's gone.'
needs.linking.because: You say it to explain how come.
Try it like this: 'I'm sad because it broke.'
needs.linking.with: You say it when one thing goes along with another.
Try it like this: 'Come with me' or 'eat with a spoon.'
needs.linking.for: 'For' shows who gets something or what it's used for.
You say it to tell who a thing is meant for.
Try it like this: 'This is for you.'
needs.linking.to: 'To' shows where you are going or who gets something.
You say it before a place or before an action.
Try it like this: 'Go to bed' or 'I want to play.'
needs.linking.helping.am: 'Am' goes with 'I' to tell what you are doing or feeling.
Try it like this: 'I am happy' or 'I am playing.'
needs.linking.helping.are: 'Are' goes with 'you', 'we', or 'they' to tell what's happening.
You say it to join those words to an action or a feeling.
Try it like this: 'You are nice' or 'We are here.'
needs.linking.helping.was: 'Was' tells about one thing that already happened.
You say it about the past.
Try it like this: 'It was fun' or 'I was there.'
needs.linking.helping.have: 'Have' means something is yours, or it helps tell what's done.
You say it about what you hold or own.
Try it like this: 'I have it' or 'I have two.'
needs.linking.helping.has: 'Has' is like 'have' but for one other person or thing.
You say it about what someone else holds or owns.
Try it like this: 'She has it' or 'He has more.'
needs.linking.helping.had: 'Had' tells about something you held or owned before.
You say it about the past.
Try it like this: 'I had one' or 'We had fun.'
needs.linking.helping.could: 'Could' means something was able to happen, or might happen.
You say it to ask nicely or to talk about a maybe.
Try it like this: 'Could I have one?'
needs.linking.helping.would: 'Would' talks about something you might like or might do.
You say it to ask politely or share a wish.
Try it like this: 'Would you help me?' or 'I would like that.'
needs.linking.conj.or: 'Or' shows a choice between two things.
You say it when you can pick one.
Try it like this: 'milk or water?' or 'this or that.'
needs.linking.conj.so: 'So' shows what happened next because of something.
You say it to connect a reason to what came after.
Try it like this: 'I was tired, so I slept.'
needs.linking.conj.if: 'If' means maybe — something happens only when something else does.
You say it to talk about a what-if.
Try it like this: 'If you want, we can go.'
needs.linking.neg.not: 'Not' makes a word mean the opposite, or says no to it.
You say it to tell what is not so.
Try it like this: 'It's not mine' or 'I'm not done.'
needs.linking.neg.dont: 'Don't' tells someone to stop, or not to do something.
You say it when you don't want something to happen.
Try it like this: 'Don't stop!' or 'I don't want that.'
needs.quant.all: 'All' means every bit, the whole thing, none left out.
You say it when you mean the entire amount.
Try it like this: 'I ate it all' or 'all of them.'
needs.quant.some: 'Some' means a part of it, not all.
You say it when you want a little, not the whole thing.
Try it like this: 'I want some' or 'some milk, please.'
needs.quant.many: 'Many' means a lot of things you can count.
You say it when there are a big bunch.
Try it like this: 'so many toys!' or 'too many.'
needs.quant.few: 'Few' means just a small number, only a couple.
You say it when there are not many.
Try it like this: 'just a few' or 'a few more.'
needs.quant.much: 'Much' is how you talk about a big amount of one thing.
You say it about stuff you don't count one by one, like water.
Try it like this: 'too much' or 'how much?'
needs.quant.any: 'Any' asks or tells about even a little bit, some at all.
You say it in questions and when something is all gone.
Try it like this: 'Any more?' or 'There isn't any.'
needs.quant.none: 'None' means not even one, all gone.
You say it when there is nothing left.
Try it like this: 'There's none left.'
needs.quant.every: 'Every' means each one, all of them one by one.
You say it when you mean every single one.
Try it like this: 'every day' or 'every one of them.'
needs.adverbs.quickly: You say it to tell that an action happens in a hurry.
Try it like this: 'Run quickly!' or 'eat quickly.'
needs.adverbs.slowly: 'Slowly' means doing something not fast, taking your time.
You say it to tell that an action happens little by little.
Try it like this: 'Walk slowly' or 'go slowly.'
needs.adverbs.carefully: 'Carefully' means doing something with care, so nothing breaks or spills.
You say it when something needs gentle attention.
Try it like this: 'Carry it carefully.'
needs.adverbs.gently: 'Gently' means in a soft, kind way, not rough.
You say it when you touch something soft and slow.
Try it like this: 'Pet the dog gently.'
needs.adverbs.loudly: 'Loudly' means making a big, strong sound.
You say it when something is hard on the ears.
Try it like this: 'He sang loudly.'
needs.adverbs.quietly: 'Quietly' means making very little sound, soft and low.
You say it when something needs to be hushed.
Try it like this: 'Talk quietly' or 'play quietly.'
needs.adverbs.always: 'Always' means every time, all the time.
You say it about something that never changes.
Try it like this: 'I always brush my teeth.'
needs.adverbs.never: 'Never' means not ever, not a single time.
You say it about something that does not happen.
Try it like this: 'I never give up.'
needs.adverbs.sometimes: 'Sometimes' means once in a while, not always.
You say it about something that happens now and then.
Try it like this: 'Sometimes we go to the park.'
needs.adverbs.almost: 'Almost' means very close, nearly there but not quite.
You say it when something is about to be true.
Try it like this: 'I'm almost done' or 'almost there!'
